text: Hi, we are back in Mumbai for another episode of This is My Architecture.
I am Abhishek from AWS and with me I have Anand from Jubilant Foodworks.
Welcome Anand.
Thank you, Abhishek.
So Anand, tell us a bit about Jubilant Foodworks.
Sure.
Jubilant Foodworks is the master franchisee for Domino's and Dunkin' Donuts in South Asia.
We've got around 1,250 restaurants in these three countries in South Asia.
We deliver around 200,000 pizzas every day to around 100,000 customers and that to under 30 minutes.
As far as I am concerned, I head the digital department in Jubilant that comprises of the digital assets and the data engineering part.
Well, that's a lot of customers you have and you're looking at one of your customers.
So every time I order a pizza, you are kind of responsible for ensuring that I get it on time.
That's fantastic.
I mean, to run a business like Jubilant Foodworks, I mean, I'm sure there are plenty of challenges, right?
So why don't you share, you know, what are those things that keep you up at night?
Sure.
There are multiple challenges that we are trying to solve by being present on the given architecture.

So one problem is, of course, the problem of scale. Since we hit around 1,000 or 1,500 orders per minute during our peak hours, ensuring that we are up during lunchtime, during dinnertime, during weekends, during festivities, that's, of course, a big challenge.
But in the context of today's discussion, the primary challenge is data availability.
I see.
Before we had our presence on AWS in the form of a data lake, one of the biggest problems was multiple versions of truth.
There would be different pipelines from each source of truth, leading to a different version of the data being available to the business stakeholders.

The second problem was data latency. Because of the latency that was inherent in the data pipelines, it would take weeks before campaign insights would be available to the marketing team and so on and so forth.
And we could, of course, not wait that long. So, and these two problems and many more we are trying to solve by being present on the data warehouse provided by AWS.
Fantastic. So, you know, why don't you take us through how you've solved these problems?
Sure.
So, Abhishek, as you can see, we've got our sources of truth laid out here.
Yeah.
We've got our POS machines, which are a point of sale systems in 1,200 restaurants.
We've got our e-commerce systems, which is our app, mobile website and desktop website here.
Any transaction that happens in the e-commerce part, which, of course, flows into our POS machines so that they can be served from the restaurants.
Yeah.
It also flows into our e-commerce database, which is a MySQL system.
Data from the POS machines that flows into our consolidation server, which is on AWS cloud.
From the e-commerce database, we use AWS database management, sorry, database migration service to take that data into a raw layer in S3, which is our data lake.
We've got our own ETL scripts that transform this data and load it into S3 from the POS database.
So, this is the raw layer which all of this data goes to.
Okay. So, I see all of your data is sitting in S3 here.
I see you're using Redshift, Redshift Spectrum, EMR here.
So, tell us how these services are being used.
Sure. So, Redshift is essentially our data warehouse.
But before we get there, what happens is that, like I said, all of this data gets dumped into a raw bucket, which is all the data dump.
Yeah.
We then use EMR jobs to move that data back into a curated layer, which is another bucket within S3.
That curated layer is used to feed third-party services as well.
But again, we use EMR to move that data from the S3 bucket, the curated layer, to Redshift as well.
Okay. So, you're loading the data there?
Yes, that's correct.
Okay.
But we load only the hot data.
Okay.
So, in the context of Jubilant, we define hot data as data that's relevant to around the last five or six months.
Okay.
Anything beyond that is cold or warm data that resides within S3.
As you can see, we've got Redshift Spectrum here as well.
And that's a huge cost-saver for us and performance enhancer as well.
So, what it does is that when a user queries Redshift for information, if all of that information is there in Redshift, well and good.
If it has to refer to data within the data lake, which is the S3 bucket, then using Spectrum, it transparently joins the data and provides it to the user and hence saving that cost.
Fantastic.
So, you get faster results and you're saving out on cost as well.
Yeah.
And the best part, it's serverless.
Yeah.
Great.
I also see you have Athena here, right?
So, while you have Redshift and Spectrum, tell us a bit about what Athena does here.
Oh, yes.
Athena has been a lifesaver.
Yeah.
And I'll tell you why.
Because, you know, there's only five or six months of data in Redshift, but years of data lie within S3.
Okay.
And the business often needs to refer to that data.
For example, year-on-year growth, quarter-on-quarter growth, and so on and so forth.
For that, the data analyst fires SQL-like queries into S3, gets that data, and provides those insights to the business.
So, all of the business insights that need to be provided on an ad hoc basis, hundreds of them every day, are powered by Athena.
Great.
So, basically, you know, what you're focusing on a day-to-day basis is the last five, six months of data.
Yeah.
That's your hot data.
You're working with.
Yeah.
So, using Redshift, and you're further optimizing it using Redshift Spectrum.
But for the business users who want to access data beyond last five, six months, you know, the data still resides in S3.
Yeah.
And you, instead of running your own infrastructure, managing your own server, you simply use Athena, which is serverless, and you get all of those data, right?
Yeah.
I think this is really, you know, great.
I mean, it's a very clever way of using various analytic services that we have.
So, what's next on the roadmap?
You know, how are you iterating over it?
Oh, yes.
We are just getting started.
Yeah.
So, we talked about a few sources of truth here.
Yeah.
Two key sources of truth, which is our HRIS, which is the human resource data that's yet to be plugged in.
And we've got our inventory data from SAP that's yet to be plugged in.
So, those are key components, and that's coming up soon in the next phase.

The other thing that I'm really looking forward to is SageMaker, because we've got all our years of data here, and that can be used as training data for the SageMaker models, which will power our forecasting engines and our personalization engines, both to be used for enhanced consumer experience.
Fantastic.
You know, makes sense.
You already have quite a bit of data in S3, and then you mentioned you're pulling in data from other sources like SAP into it as well.
Yeah.
And of course, with SageMaker, it's really easy to build, train, and deploy your model, and get more insights, and do forecasting on it, right?
This is fantastic, Anand.
I learned a lot about your architecture today.
Thank you for coming and sharing all these details with me.
Thank you.
And thank you for watching This is My Architecture.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Thank you.
